walkthrough.title: Unfold the Game Mat
walkthrough.text: Let's get Onitama set up. First, unfold the game mat and place it between the two players. The mat shows a five-by-five grid with special markings on the center spaces of each player's back row. Those marked spaces are the temple arches, and they are important for one of the win conditions.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Color
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color, either red or blue. Take all five pawns of your chosen color. You will notice that one pawn is taller or differently shaped than the other four. That tall piece is your master. The four smaller pieces are your students.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Pawns
walkthrough.text: Place your five pawns on the back row closest to you. Your master goes on the center space of your back row, which is the temple arch. Your four students fill the remaining four spaces on that same row. Your opponent does the same on their side.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Move Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the sixteen move cards. Each card shows a unique movement pattern on a small grid. Give the whole deck a good shuffle. Only five of these sixteen cards will be used in any given game, so every match feels different.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Move Cards
walkthrough.text: Deal two move cards face up to each player. Then flip one more card face up and place it to the side of the board. This fifth card is called the waiting card. Put the remaining eleven cards back in the box without looking at them. They will not be used this game.

walkthrough.title: Determine the Starting Player
walkthrough.text: Look at the waiting card you placed beside the board. It has a color stamp on it, either red or blue. Whichever player matches that color goes first. All five cards are always face up and visible to both players. There is no hidden information in Onitama. You are ready to play!

summary.title: Unfold the Game Mat
bullets: Place the 5x5 game mat between both players
Center spaces on each back row are temple arches

summary.title: Choose Your Color
bullets: Each player takes 5 pawns of one color (red or blue)
1 master (taller piece) + 4 students (smaller pieces)

summary.title: Place Your Pawns
bullets: All 5 pawns go on your back row
Master on center space (temple arch), students on remaining 4 spaces

summary.title: Shuffle the Move Cards
bullets: Shuffle all 16 move cards
Only 5 will be used per game — rest go back in the box

summary.title: Deal the Move Cards
bullets: Deal 2 cards face up to each player
Place 1 card face up beside the board (the waiting card)
Return remaining 11 cards to box unseen

summary.title: Determine the Starting Player
bullets: Color stamp on the waiting card determines who goes first
All 5 cards are always visible — no hidden information

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Onitama is a chess-like game for two players on a five-by-five grid. Instead of each piece having its own movement rules, you use movement cards that tell you how any piece can move. The cards rotate between players, so the move you use this turn will eventually end up in your opponent's hands.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn in Three Steps
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do three things. First, choose one of your two move cards. Second, move one of your pieces according to that card's pattern. Third, swap the used card with the waiting card. That is the entire turn. Let's break down each step.

walkthrough.title: Reading a Move Card
walkthrough.text: Each move card shows a small grid with a center square and several highlighted squares. The center square represents the piece you are moving. The highlighted squares show the spaces that piece can move to, relative to its current position. The pattern is oriented from your perspective, so up on the card means forward toward your opponent.

walkthrough.title: Making a Move
walkthrough.text: Pick any one of your five pieces, master or student. Then pick one of the highlighted spaces from the card you chose. Move your piece to the corresponding space on the board. If the destination is off the edge of the board, that particular option is not available. You cannot land on a space occupied by one of your own pieces.

walkthrough.title: Capturing
walkthrough.text: If you move one of your pieces onto a space occupied by an opponent's piece, you capture it. Remove the captured piece from the board. It is gone for the rest of the game. Both masters and students can capture, and both can be captured. The only exception is that capturing the opponent's master ends the game immediately.

walkthrough.title: The Card Swap
walkthrough.text: After you move, take the card you just used and place it to the side of the board. It becomes the new waiting card. Then pick up the old waiting card and add it to your hand. You always have exactly two cards in front of you. This rotation mechanic means every card cycles between both players over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: No Passing Allowed
walkthrough.text: You must make a move on your turn if any legal move is possible. Look at both of your cards and all of your pieces. If at least one combination results in a valid move, you must take it. In the very rare case where no legal move exists with either card, you still must choose a card and swap it with the waiting card, but you do not move any piece.

walkthrough.title: Way of the Stone
walkthrough.text: There are two ways to win. The first is called Way of the Stone. If you capture your opponent's master, you win immediately. This is the most common victory condition. Protecting your master while hunting your opponent's master is the core tension of the game.

walkthrough.title: Way of the Stream
walkthrough.text: The second win condition is called Way of the Stream. If you move your master onto your opponent's temple arch, which is the center space of their back row, you win immediately. This means your opponent must guard their temple even while attacking yours. Always keep both win conditions in mind.

walkthrough.title: Perfect Information
walkthrough.text: One important thing about Onitama is that it has perfect information. All five move cards are always face up and visible to both players. You can see exactly which moves your opponent can make right now, and you can see which card is waiting for the next turn. Use this information to plan ahead and think about what your opponent will do.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: 2-player chess-like game on a 5x5 grid
Movement cards define how pieces move (not piece types)
Cards rotate between players after each use

2. Move one piece according to the card's pattern
3. Swap used card with the waiting card

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Dojo
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through several turns of a scripted match between two players, Red and Blue. You will see how movement cards work, how capturing happens, and how the card swap creates interesting decisions. Follow along on your own board if you like.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Both players have five pieces on their back row. Each master sits on the center space, which is also their temple arch. Each player has two move cards face up in front of them, and one waiting card sits beside the board. Take a moment to look at all five cards and understand the movement options available.

walkthrough.title: Red's First Move: Advance a Student
walkthrough.text: Red picks a move card that allows moving one space forward. Red moves a student from the back row toward the center of the board. This is a safe opening because students are expendable. Red then swaps the used card with the waiting card.

walkthrough.title: Why Students Go First
walkthrough.text: Notice that Red moved a student, not the master. In Onitama, your master is your most valuable piece. If it gets captured, you lose immediately. Leading with students lets you control space and threaten captures without risking the game. Think of students as scouts.

walkthrough.title: Blue's First Move: Mirror Advance
walkthrough.text: Blue also advances a student toward the center. Both players are jockeying for position in the middle of the board. Center control is important because it gives your pieces more movement options. Pieces near the edges have fewer valid destinations since moves off the board are not allowed.

walkthrough.title: Red's Mistake: Exposing the Master
walkthrough.text: Red gets aggressive and moves the master forward two spaces using a long-range card. The master is now in the middle of the board with no students protecting it. This is a classic beginner mistake. The master is exposed to captures from multiple directions.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Keep Your Master Safe
walkthrough.text: Moving your master into open space early is dangerous. Your opponent can see all the cards, so they know exactly how to reach your master. Keep your master behind your students in the early game. Only advance the master when you have a clear purpose, like threatening Way of the Stream or when the path is safe.

walkthrough.title: Blue Threatens a Capture
walkthrough.text: Blue sees that Red's master is exposed. Blue checks both of their move cards and finds one that can reach Red's master in one move. But wait, Blue also checks the waiting card. If Blue uses that card for the capture, what card does Red get next turn? Always think about what cards your opponent will receive.

walkthrough.title: The Card You Give Matters
walkthrough.text: Blue decides to capture Red's exposed student instead of going straight for the master. Why? Because the card that would reach the master has a powerful movement pattern. If Blue uses it and swaps it to the waiting position, Red would pick it up next turn. Sometimes the right play is a smaller capture that keeps a dangerous card out of your opponent's hands.

walkthrough.title: Red's Mistake: Ignoring the Temple
walkthrough.text: Red retreats the master back but ignores the temple arch completely. Meanwhile, Blue has been quietly advancing a student along the side of the board. That student is now only one move away from Red's temple arch. Red did not notice the Way of the Stream threat.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch Both Win Conditions
walkthrough.text: New players often focus entirely on capturing pieces and forget about Way of the Stream. Always check whether your opponent's master can reach your temple arch on their next turn. Also watch for your own opportunities to sneak your master across the board. The best players threaten both win conditions at the same time.

walkthrough.title: Blue Creates a Fork
walkthrough.text: Blue positions a student so that next turn, Blue can either capture Red's master or move Blue's own master toward the temple arch. Red can only block one of these threats. This is called a fork, and it is one of the most powerful tactics in Onitama. When you threaten two things at once, your opponent cannot stop both.

walkthrough.title: Red's Mistake: Forgetting the Card Cycle
walkthrough.text: Red sees the fork and panics. Red uses a card to block one threat, but does not check what card will cycle to Blue from the waiting position. The new card Blue picks up is exactly what Blue needs to execute the other threat. Red's block failed because Red forgot to track the card rotation.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Read Ahead
walkthrough.text: Before you commit to a move, look at the waiting card. Ask yourself what card your opponent will get after your turn. In Onitama, thinking one full turn ahead, your move plus your opponent's response, is the minimum. If the waiting card enables your opponent to do something dangerous, consider using a different card even if it means a less exciting move.

walkthrough.title: Blue Wins
walkthrough.text: Blue takes advantage of the fork and moves the master to Red's temple arch. Way of the Stream! Blue wins. Red made three common mistakes: exposing the master early, ignoring the temple threat, and forgetting to track the card cycle. Let's review what we learned.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your own game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all the movement cards and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick guide to move cards and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, rushing the master forward too early. Your master is the most valuable piece, and exposing it invites a quick loss. Second, ignoring Way of the Stream. Many beginners focus only on capturing pieces and forget that a master sneaking to the temple arch wins the game. Third, not tracking the card rotation. Every card you use goes to your opponent eventually. Fourth, treating all pieces the same. Your master needs more protection than your students.

walkthrough.title: Control the Center
walkthrough.text: The center of the five-by-five board is the most valuable real estate. Pieces in the center have maximum flexibility because none of their movement options get cut off by the board edges. A student in the center can threaten captures in multiple directions, while a piece stuck in a corner has very few valid moves. In the opening, advance students toward the center to establish control before committing your master to any direction.

walkthrough.title: Think About the Card Swap
walkthrough.text: The card rotation is what makes Onitama unique. Before you use a card, ask yourself two questions. First, does the waiting card I will pick up give me something useful next turn? Second, does the card I am giving up help my opponent more than it helps me right now? Sometimes it is correct to make a mediocre move just to keep a powerful card out of your opponent's hands. Other times, you should use your best card aggressively because the waiting card you pick up is even better.

walkthrough.title: Read One Turn Ahead
walkthrough.text: Because all cards are face up, you can calculate exactly what your opponent can do on their next turn. Before you move, look at their two cards and check every possible move they could make. Can they capture your master? Can their master reach your temple? If the answer to either question is yes, you must deal with that threat. This one-turn lookahead is the minimum thinking required for solid play.

walkthrough.title: Create Forks
walkthrough.text: A fork is when you threaten two things at once and your opponent can only stop one. The most powerful fork in Onitama is threatening both Way of the Stone and Way of the Stream simultaneously. If your opponent blocks the master capture, your master walks into their temple. If they guard the temple, you capture their master. Setting up forks requires patience and positioning, but they are the most reliable way to win.

walkthrough.title: Use Students as Shields and Threats
walkthrough.text: Students are expendable, but they are not worthless. Use them to block your opponent's path to your master and to your temple arch. A well-placed student can force your opponent to waste a turn capturing it instead of advancing toward a win condition. Students can also deliver captures. A student taking out an opposing student near the center can swing the balance of the game.

walkthrough.title: Adapt to the Cards
walkthrough.text: Every game of Onitama uses a different set of five cards, so there is no single opening strategy that always works. Before your first move, study all five cards. Are the movement patterns mostly short-range or long-range? Are there diagonal cards or mostly straight-line cards? Adapt your plan to the cards in play. If the cards favor long-range movement, keep your master far back. If the cards are mostly short-range, center control becomes even more important.

walkthrough.title: Don't Trade Pieces Evenly
walkthrough.text: When you have more students than your opponent, you have an advantage. More pieces means more options and more threats. Do not trade students one-for-one unless the exchange gives you a positional advantage. Instead, try to capture an opponent's piece for free without losing one of your own. If you can get ahead by even one student, your opponent will struggle to defend both win conditions with fewer pieces.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't rush master forward — it invites quick capture
Don't ignore Way of the Stream — temple wins are real
Track the card rotation — your used card goes to opponent
Protect master more than students — losing master = losing game

bullets: Center pieces have maximum movement flexibility
Edge/corner pieces lose movement options off the board
Advance students to center first, then commit master

summary.title: Think About the Card Swap
bullets: Ask: does the waiting card help me next turn?
Ask: does my used card help my opponent more than me?
Sometimes make a weaker move to deny opponent a strong card

summary.title: Read One Turn Ahead
bullets: All cards are face up — calculate opponent's every possible move
Can they capture your master? Can they reach your temple?
If yes to either → deal with the threat before anything else

summary.title: Create Forks
bullets: Fork = threaten 2 things at once, opponent can only block 1
Best fork: Way of the Stone + Way of the Stream simultaneously
Requires patience and positioning but wins games reliably

summary.title: Use Students as Shields and Threats
bullets: Block paths to your master and temple with students
Force opponent to waste turns capturing students
Students can also deliver key captures for positional advantage

summary.title: Adapt to the Cards
bullets: Every game uses a different set of 5 cards — no fixed opening
Study all 5 cards before your first move
Long-range cards → keep master back; short-range → center control

summary.title: Don't Trade Pieces Evenly
bullets: More students = more options and threats
Avoid 1-for-1 trades unless you gain positional advantage
Being up even 1 student makes defending both win conditions harder for opponent

walkthrough.title: Card Denial
walkthrough.text: In Onitama, you are not just playing your cards. You are also controlling which cards your opponent gets. If one of the five cards has a particularly strong movement pattern, think carefully about when and how you use it. Sometimes it is worth using a powerful card for a minor move just to send it to the waiting position at the right time. If the timing works out, your opponent might pick up a card they cannot use effectively on their next turn.

walkthrough.title: Tempo and Pacing
walkthrough.text: Tempo in Onitama means making moves that force your opponent to react rather than execute their own plan. A player with tempo dictates the flow of the game. You gain tempo by creating threats that must be answered. You lose tempo when you have to respond to your opponent's threats instead of advancing your own position. If you find yourself only reacting, try to make a move that threatens two things at once to regain the initiative.

walkthrough.title: The Waiting Card Trap
walkthrough.text: An advanced tactic is using the waiting card position to set a trap. Suppose the waiting card has a long diagonal leap. You can position your master one turn away from your opponent's temple, but only reachable with that specific card. On your current turn, you use a card and pick up something else. Your opponent sees the threat but cannot stop it because the card you need is cycling through the waiting position and will arrive in your hand at just the right moment.

walkthrough.title: Two-Turn Combinations
walkthrough.text: Strong players think at least two turns ahead. A two-turn combination means your first move sets up a position where your second move delivers the win, and your opponent cannot stop it regardless of what they do in between. To find these, look at the card you will pick up from the waiting position and ask: if I make this move now, and then use that card next turn, does it create an unstoppable threat?

walkthrough.title: Positional Squeezes
walkthrough.text: A positional squeeze happens when you push your opponent's pieces to the edges and corners of the board. Edge pieces have severely limited movement options because any move that goes off the board is invalid. If you can force your opponent's master into a corner, they may have no good moves available. Meanwhile, your centrally positioned pieces have full access to all their movement patterns. Corner traps are especially deadly because sometimes neither card offers a useful escape.

walkthrough.title: Sacrifice Tactics
walkthrough.text: Sometimes offering a student as a sacrifice is the strongest move. If capturing your student forces your opponent to move a piece into a bad position, or forces them to use a card they wanted to save, the trade is worth it. Before sacrificing, ask three questions. Does my opponent have to take the bait? What card do they need to use to capture? And does the resulting position benefit me more than losing a student hurts? The best sacrifices set up a fork or a two-turn combination.

walkthrough.title: Card Pattern Awareness
walkthrough.text: Over many games, you will start to recognize which cards are strongest and weakest. Cards with many movement options give flexibility but telegraph your intentions. Cards with fewer options are more limited but harder for your opponent to predict. Diagonal movement cards are especially tricky because new players tend to think in straight lines. Learn to recognize when a diagonal card opens up a surprise capture or temple approach that your opponent did not see coming.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Conversion
walkthrough.text: When you have a material advantage, meaning more students than your opponent, shift your focus to converting that advantage into a win. With more pieces, you can threaten multiple targets simultaneously while your opponent lacks the pieces to defend everything. Use your extra students to create diversions and screen your master's approach toward their temple. The fewer pieces your opponent has, the harder it is for them to create counter-threats. Be methodical and patient in the endgame rather than rushing for a flashy finish.

summary.title: Card Denial
bullets: Control which cards opponent receives via the swap
Use a strong card for a minor move to deny it at the right time
Time swaps so opponent picks up cards they can't use effectively

summary.title: Tempo and Pacing
bullets: Tempo = forcing opponent to react instead of executing their plan
Create threats that must be answered to gain tempo
If stuck reacting, create a dual threat to regain initiative

summary.title: The Waiting Card Trap
bullets: Use card cycling timing to set up unavoidable threats
Position pieces so the card you need arrives at the perfect moment
Opponent sees the threat but can't prevent the card from cycling

summary.title: Two-Turn Combinations
bullets: Plan: this move now + waiting card next turn = unstoppable threat
Check if opponent can disrupt the sequence between turns
Strong combos win games even against skilled opponents

summary.title: Positional Squeezes
bullets: Push opponent's pieces to edges/corners where movement is limited
Corner-trapped masters may have no useful escape moves
Central pieces maintain full access to all movement patterns

summary.title: Sacrifice Tactics
bullets: Offer a student to force opponent into a bad position or card use
Ask: must they take it? What card do they use? Does the result favor me?
Best sacrifices set up forks or two-turn combos

summary.title: Card Pattern Awareness
bullets: Many-option cards = flexible but telegraph intentions
Few-option cards = limited but harder to predict
Diagonal cards create surprise angles most players overlook

summary.title: Endgame Conversion
bullets: Material advantage (more students) → convert to a win
Use extra students as diversions and screens for master advance
Fewer opponent pieces = harder for them to create counter-threats
Be methodical — don't rush for a flashy finish

entries.definition: The taller pawn that starts on the center space of your back row (the temple arch). If your master is captured, you lose. If your master reaches the opponent's temple arch, you win.

The four smaller pawns that start on the remaining back row spaces. Students move and capture the same as the master but losing them does not end the game. They serve as scouts, shields, and attackers.
The center space of each player's back row, marked on the game mat. Your master starts here. If the opponent's master reaches your temple arch, they win via Way of the Stream.
The five-by-five grid where the game is played. Made of neoprene or cloth, it folds out between the two players. Temple arches are marked on the center of each back row.

A card showing a movement pattern on a small grid. The center square represents the moving piece. Highlighted squares show valid destinations relative to the piece. There are sixteen unique move cards in the game, but only five are used per match.
The fifth move card placed beside the board. After you use a card, it replaces the waiting card, and you pick up the old waiting card. This creates the rotation that cycles cards between players.

The third step of every turn. Your used card goes to the waiting position, and you take the previous waiting card into your hand. You always have exactly two cards. Your opponent will eventually receive every card you use.
One of two win conditions. Capture your opponent's master to win immediately. This is the most common victory method and the primary threat to watch for throughout the game.

One of two win conditions. Move your master onto the opponent's temple arch (center of their back row) to win immediately. This alternate win condition forces both players to defend their temples while attacking.
A position where you threaten two things simultaneously and your opponent can only block one. The strongest fork threatens both Way of the Stone and Way of the Stream at the same time.
Using a card for a minor move to control when your opponent receives it. By timing your card swaps, you can prevent your opponent from having the right card at the right moment.
Having the initiative by making moves that force your opponent to react. A player with tempo dictates the flow of the game while their opponent is stuck defending.
Move card. Allows a two-space jump forward or one space backward. A strong aggressive card for charging toward the opponent.
Move card. Allows wide diagonal moves — two spaces diagonally forward-left or forward-right, and one space diagonally backward-left or backward-right. Great for flanking.
Move card. Allows one space forward or two spaces to either side. Good for lateral movement and controlling the center row.
Move card. Allows one space in any diagonal direction. Versatile card with four movement options covering all diagonal angles.
Move card. Allows one space forward or one space diagonally backward in either direction. Balanced between offense and retreat.
Move card. Allows one space diagonally forward-left, one space diagonally forward-right, or one space straight backward. Good for aggressive play with a retreat option.
Move card. Allows one space forward, one space to the left, or one space backward. Limited but useful for lateral positioning.
Move card. Allows one space forward or one space to either side. A solid defensive card for lateral control.
Move card. Allows one space diagonally forward-left, one space diagonally forward-right, one space left, or one space right. Four-option card with strong forward and lateral coverage.
Move card. Allows one space right, one space left, one space diagonally forward-left, or one space diagonally backward-right. Diagonal cross pattern.
Move card. Allows one space diagonally forward-left, two spaces to the left, or one space diagonally backward-right. Asymmetric left-leaning card.
Move card. Allows one space to the left, one space diagonally forward-right, or one space diagonally backward-right. Right-side focused with a left step.
Move card. Allows one space to the right, one space diagonally forward-left, or one space diagonally backward-left. Mirror of Cobra, left-side focused with a right step.
